perturbation.original_text: Company agrees, upon request, to promptly return or destroy all items relating to Confidential Information, including but not limited to, all materials, written materials, drawings, data and records provided or otherwise made available by Verenium, and all copies (in any form) thereof, with a certificate of compliance with the foregoing, executed by an appropriate officer of Company within 30 days of such request, provided that the Company may maintain a single copy of such Confidential Information within its Legal Department for archival purposes.
perturbation.changed_text: Company agrees to make reasonable efforts to return or destroy items relating to Confidential Information, including materials, written materials, drawings, data, and records provided or made available by Verenium, and copies thereof. A certificate of compliance may be provided, if practical, within a reasonable timeframe of such request. The Company may maintain a copy of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces 'promptly return or destroy all items' with 'make reasonable efforts to return or destroy items', making the obligation less strict. It also makes the certificate of compliance optional ('may be provided, if practical') and removes the specific 30-day timeframe, further weakening the obligation. This introduces ambiguity, as it is unclear what constitutes 'reasonable efforts' or a 'reasonable timeframe'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Trade Secret Act allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information and making compliance optional, the modified text makes it more difficult for Verenium to obtain an injunction if Company fails to adequately protect the confidential information, potentially including trade secrets, upon request, thus hindering the protection afforded by the law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426.2 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.2. (a) Actual or threatened misappropriation may be enjoined. Upon application to the court, an injunction shall be terminated when the trade secret has ceased to exist, but the injunction may be continued for an additional period of time in order to eliminate commercial advantage that otherwise would be derived from the misappropriation. (b) If the court determines that it would be unreasonable to prohibit future use, an injunction may condition future use upon payment of a reasonable royalty for no longer than the period of time the use could have been prohibited. (c) In appropriate circumstances, affirmative acts to protect a trade secret may be compelled by court order. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities regarding the return or destruction of confidential information, weakening the original obligation. The original text requires prompt action and a certificate of compliance within 30 days, whereas the changed text only requires 'reasonable efforts' and an optional certificate of compliance within a 'reasonable timeframe'. This weakening undermines the protections afforded by California Code, CIV 3426.2(a), which allows for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the obligation to return or destroy information less strict, it becomes more difficult to obtain an injunction if the company fails to adequately protect confidential information.

perturbation.original_text: During the one-year period commencing on the date of this Agreement (the “Standstill Period”), neither the Company nor any of its Representatives will, in any manner, directly or indirectly (except to the extent agreed by Verenium): (a) make, effect, initiate, cause or participate in (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of Verenium or any securities of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, in excess of five (5) percent of the outstanding securities of any such company (with any such acquisition, regardless of size, only being made at such time as neither the acquiring party nor any of its Affiliates is in possession of material, non-public information about Verenium)
perturbation.changed_text: During the one-year period commencing on the date of this Agreement (the “Standstill Period”), neither the Company nor any of its Representatives will endeavor, in any manner, directly or indirectly (except to the extent agreed by Verenium): (a) make, effect, initiate, cause or participate in (i) any acquisition of beneficial ownership of any securities of Verenium or any securities of any subsidiary or other Affiliate of Verenium, potentially in excess of five (5) percent of the outstanding securities of any such company (with any such acquisition, regardless of size, possibly being made even if the acquiring party or any of its Affiliates is in possession of material, non-public information about Verenium)
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'will' with 'will endeavor', and using the terms 'potentially' and 'possibly', the firmness of the standstill agreement is reduced. This creates ambiguity about the level of commitment required, especially regarding actions like acquisitions while possessing non-public information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934 and Rule 10b-5 prohibit the use of manipulative and deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. The modifications create an ambiguity suggesting that acquisitions might be permissible even with material non-public information, contradicting the strict prohibitions against insider trading.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity that undermines the prohibition against using material non-public information, as described in Section 10(b) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934. By changing 'will' to 'will endeavor' and adding 'potentially' and 'possibly', the altered contract suggests that acquiring securities with insider information might be permissible, weakening the original commitment. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 15 USC 78j concerning 'manipulative and deceptive devices,' supports the argument that such ambiguity could facilitate actions that violate securities laws, as it makes the legal obligation difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: Company acknowledges that it is aware, and will advise its Representatives who are informed of the Purpose or who review Confidential Information, of the restrictions imposed by the United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of Verenium’s securities by any person who has received material, non-public information about Verenium and on the communication of such information to any other person when it is reasonably foreseeable that such other person is likely to purchase or sell such securities in reliance upon such information.
perturbation.changed_text: Company acknowledges that it is generally aware, and may advise its Representatives who are exposed to the Purpose or who might review Confidential Information, of the potential restrictions imposed by United States securities laws on the purchase or sale of Verenium’s securities by those who have possibly received material, non-public information and on the communication of such information when it could be foreseeable that such a person might purchase or sell such securities.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces several ambiguities. Replacing 'is aware' with 'is generally aware' dilutes the certainty of the Company's knowledge. 'Will advise' becomes 'may advise,' making the dissemination of crucial legal information discretionary. 'Are informed' becomes 'are exposed to' and 'who review' becomes 'might review', reducing the specificity of who receives the advice. Changing 'restrictions imposed' to 'potential restrictions' weakens the legal impact, and replacing 'has received' with 'has possibly received' lowers the threshold for triggering securities law restrictions. Finally, 'is reasonably foreseeable' is softened to 'could be foreseeable'.
perturbation.law_explanation: SEC Rule 10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud; making any untrue statement of a material fact or omitting to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading; or engaging in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By creating uncertainty regarding the Company's obligation to inform its representatives of insider trading restrictions, the modified text weakens the safeguard against illegal insider trading activities, conflicting with the intent of Rule 10b-5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces ambiguities that weaken the company's obligation to inform its representatives about insider trading restrictions, potentially conflicting with the intent of SEC Rule 10b-5. The original contract stated the Company 'is aware' and 'will advise', creating a firm obligation. The modified contract uses softer language like 'is generally aware' and 'may advise', making the obligation less stringent. The law snippet 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 prohibits employing any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By weakening the obligation to inform representatives, the modified text creates a loophole where representatives might not be fully aware of insider trading restrictions, potentially leading to violations of Rule 10b-5. Therefore, the modified text creates an ambiguity that contradicts the intent to prevent fraud.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents and warrants that it has the authority to undertake the obligations set forth in this Agreement without breaching or violating any contractual or statutory obligation owed to another.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party believes it has the authority to undertake most of the obligations outlined in this Agreement, aiming not to breach any significant contractual obligation to another.
perturbation.explanation: The change replaces 'represents and warrants' with 'believes it has the authority', making the assurance weaker and subjective. Replacing 'the obligations' with 'most of the obligations' creates uncertainty about which obligations are fully covered. Replacing 'without breaching or violating any contractual or statutory obligation' with 'aiming not to breach any significant contractual obligation' weakens the commitment and introduces ambiguity by suggesting potential minor breaches are acceptable and excluding statutory obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California law defines a contract as an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing. The original clause provides a clear warranty against breaching other obligations. By introducing ambiguity about the intent to avoid breaching 'significant' obligations only, the modified clause weakens the commitment to fulfill contractual obligations and opens the door to potential breaches, hindering the injured party from seeking remedy under breach of contract law. This impacts the enforceability of the contract itself due to the erosion of assurances.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1549 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF A CONTRACT [1549 - 1615] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Definition [1549 - 1550.5] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 1549. A contract is an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract provides a clear representation and warranty, while the modified version weakens this commitment by introducing subjective language ('believes it has the authority') and limiting the scope of obligations ('most of the obligations'). The original clause guarantees no breach of any contractual or statutory obligation, whereas the modified clause only aims to avoid breaching 'significant' contractual obligations, effectively excluding statutory obligations and allowing for potential minor breaches. This contradicts the basic premise of a contract, as defined in California Civil Code Section 1549 ('A contract is an agreement to do or not to do a certain thing'), because the modified clause weakens the agreement to fulfill all contractual obligations, creating ambiguity.
